content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 7, 2003

The Honorable Gene Mullin
229 Alta Loma Drive
South San Francisco, CA 94080

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-339

Dear Assemblymember Mullin:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Under the Political Reform Act, is it permissible for you to hire your adult son,
Kevin Mullin, as your ongoing-political director while you serve as an officeholder in the
state Assembly?

CONCLUSION

Under the Act, campaign funds may be used to pay for the cost of professional
services rendered to a committee that are directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. Therefore, it is permissible under the Act for your committee to
compensate your son, Kevin Mullin, for professional services he provides to the
committee as its ongoing political director.

FACTS

You are requesting written advice as to whether it is permissible under the Act for
you to hire your son Kevin Mullin as your on-going political director while you serve as
an officeholder in the state Assembly. As political director, he would act as a liaison with
fundraising staff, provide political advice, and coordinate future political activities. The
position is totally separate and apart from your state staff operation, and will be paid by
campaign committee funds.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-339
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act's provisions concerning the permissible uses of
campaign funds are found in sections 89510-89522, copy enclosed. Under these
provisions, campaign funds are regarded as totally separate and distinct from a candidate
or officeholder's personal funds. The gist of the Act's campaign funds provisions is to
ensure that campaign funds are used for electioneering and officeholder purposes, and are
not expended for the candidate or officeholder's personal purposes.

At the outset, the Act provides that all contributions deposited into a campaign
account are "held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate or for
expenses associated with holding office." (Section 89510.) Generally speaking, an
expenditure related to seeking or holding office is permissible under the Act if it is
reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.)
Certain specified uses of campaign funds, including those that are considered to confer a
"substantial personal benefit" on the candidate must meet a higher standard and must be
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.)

Although we conclude in the footnote below that payment by your committee to
Mr. Mullin for his services as political advisor does not confer a "substantial personal
benefit" on you, the payment for professional services must still meet the higher standard
of being directly related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose under section
89513(b). Section 89513(b) provides as follows:

"(b) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay for or reimburse the cost
of professional services unless the services are directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(1) Expenditures by a committee to pay for professional services
reasonably required by the committee to assist it in the performance of its
administrative functions are directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose."

2 "Substantial personal benefit" is defined to mean "an expenditure of campaign funds which
results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than two hundred dollars ($200) to a candidate,
elected officer, or any individual or individual(s) with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign
funds held by a committee." (§ 89511(b)(3).) Moreover, regulation 18960 provides that an expenditure of
campaign funds results in a "direct personal benefit" when a member of the candidate's or elected official's
immediate family realizes an increase in his or her income of more than $200 from the expenditure. (Reg.
18960(a)(1).) Section 82029 of the Act defines "immediate family" to mean an official's spouse and
dependent children. A child is considered a "dependent child" for purposes of the Act if the child is under
18 years of age and the elected official is entitled to claim the dependent child as such for income tax
purposes. (Tremlett Advice Letter, No. 1-89-386.) Your son, Kevin Mullin, is over the age of 18, and
therefore, he is not considered a member of your "immediate family" under this definition. Consequently,
cash payments of $200 or more made to your adult son to compensate him for services as political director
are not considered to confer a direct personal benefit on you.

File No. A-02-339
Page No. 3

We have advised the compensation paid to a campaign worker must be
comparable to the services performed or a gift may result. (Harden Advice Letter, No.
A-90-498.) Under section 89513(b), so long as your son, Kevin Mullin, performs duties
as a political director that are directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose, and is paid fair market value for those services, the payment for his professional
services by the committee will be permissible.³

In addition, section 89513(b)(2) provides that campaign funds may not be used to
pay health-related expenses for a member of the candidate's household. You may,
however, use campaign funds to pay the employer costs of a health plan or health benefits
for a bona fide employee or independent contractor of the committee. Accordingly, the
committee may pay for health benefits for Mr. Mullin if he is hired as a bona fide
employee or independent contractor of the committee. Further, Mr. Mullin is not
considered a member of your "household," which is defined for purposes of Article 4
(campaign funds), to include "the candidate's or elected officer's spouse, dependent
children, and parents who reside with the candidate or elected officer." (Section
89511(b)(4).)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

HPW:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\02-339

³ Please note that campaign funds may not be used to compensate you for the performance of similar duties
for your own campaign committee. (Section 89518.)

sections.question:
Under the Political Reform Act, is it permissible for you to hire your adult son,
Kevin Mullin, as your ongoing-political director while you serve as an officeholder in the
state Assembly?

Under the Act, campaign funds may be used to pay for the cost of professional
services rendered to a committee that are directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. Therefore, it is permissible under the Act for your committee to
compensate your son, Kevin Mullin, for professional services he provides to the
committee as its ongoing political director.

CONCLUSION: Under the Act, campaign funds may be used to pay for the cost of professional
services rendered to a committee that are directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. Therefore, it is permissible under the Act for your committee to
